construct: Structural legitimacy engineering vs. naked power seizure
positive_pole: Embeds power transfer within procedural and legal forms so that authority is self-validating from the moment of its creation
negative_pole: Seizes power through visible force alone, leaving the question of legitimacy as an unsolved post-hoc problem
behavioral_implication: When Napoleon acquires a new institution or territory, he will invest significant effort in constructing formal ratification mechanisms — elections, codes, concordats — even when raw force would suffice in the short term, because he treats legitimacy architecture as a load-bearing structural requirement rather than an optional veneer

construct: Agreement as transitional leverage state vs. agreement as terminal endpoint
positive_pole: Treats a signed agreement as the moment of maximum asymmetric leverage over the counterparty's exit options, and uses that window to extract residual concessions
negative_pole: Treats a signed agreement as the resolution of a negotiation, after which implementation occurs within the agreed terms
behavioral_implication: When Napoleon concludes a formal treaty or concordat, he will move immediately after signing to append conditions, insert administrative controls, or extract behavioral commitments that the counterparty's sunk costs and public commitments now prevent them from rejecting — the signature is the beginning of the extraction, not the end of it

construct: Enemy belief state as a shapeable physical asset vs. enemy force as a fixed constraint to overcome
positive_pole: Treats the opponent's confidence, expectations, and interpretive frame as a malleable variable that can be actively engineered to serve as a force-multiplier
negative_pole: Treats enemy forces, positions, and ratios as the primary operative variables, and designs responses to match or exceed them directly
behavioral_implication: When Napoleon faces a numerically or positionally inferior situation, he will invest in manufacturing a specific false belief in the enemy's mind before committing forces, because a correctly engineered enemy belief state is worth more to him than an equivalent increase in his own troop strength

construct: Institutional architecture as an engineered incentive system vs. institutional architecture as a moral or natural order
positive_pole: Designs institutions — honor hierarchies, salary dependencies, legal codes — as deliberate incentive machines whose loyalty output is produced by structural positioning rather than sentiment
negative_pole: Inherits or accepts institutional structures as natural or just orders, and manages them through appeals to duty, gratitude, or tradition
behavioral_implication: When Napoleon needs to bind a powerful, proud, or potentially hostile actor to his system, he will not rely on flattery or ideological conversion but will instead restructure the actor's resource flows and advancement pathways so that their rational self-interest continuously reproduces loyalty as a behavioral output — he engineers the dependency before he requires the compliance

construct: Deliberate domain-shift when current competition dimension is unwinnable vs. persistence on the losing dimension
positive_pole: When the current competitive dimension favors the adversary, redefines the battlefield itself — relocating the contest to a domain where his own strengths apply
negative_pole: Accepts the adversary's chosen dimension of competition as fixed and attempts to overcome the disadvantage by increasing effort or resources within that dimension
behavioral_implication: When Napoleon encounters a structural asymmetry he cannot overcome on its own terms — naval inferiority, legal illegitimacy, numerical weakness — he will not simply try harder but will reframe the problem to shift the competitive axis to economic coercion, procedural legitimacy, or psychological confrontation, whichever domain converts his existing assets into the relevant currency

construct: Centralized high-throughput control as coherence guarantee vs. delegation as scalability solution
positive_pole: Maintains himself as the singular decision bottleneck and compensates through extreme personal processing speed, treating intermediary nodes as fidelity-degradation mechanisms
negative_pole: Distributes authority to ministers and commanders as the solution to scale, accepting some strategic incoherence as the acceptable cost of scalability
behavioral_implication: When Napoleon's operational scope expands — more territory, more theaters, more subordinates — he will not redesign toward distributed authority but will instead extend the centralized model and increase his own personal work rate, because he experiences delegation as a quality-control failure rather than a management solution; this will hold even when the theater's information latency has structurally broken the model's assumptions

construct: Context-invariant method application vs. boundary-condition sensitivity in proven methods
positive_pole: Exports a validated method wholesale into a new theater, treating prior success as evidence of universality rather than context-dependence
negative_pole: Audits whether the conditions that made a method succeed are actually present in the new context before committing to the method
behavioral_implication: When Napoleon deploys a method — corps logistics, centralized command, regime-installation formula — that has succeeded in multiple prior contexts, he will apply it to the new situation without systematically checking whether the invisible enabling conditions (short feedback loops, local elite collaboration, high-functioning marshal corps) are still present; the more consistently the method has succeeded, the more confident and therefore the more blind to context-shift he will be

construct: Asymmetric reversibility as the primary decision variable vs. honor or duty as the primary decision variable
positive_pole: Evaluates choices by the comparative recovery profiles of their failure modes — which loss is recoverable and which permanently forecloses future options — and acts on whichever path preserves optionality
negative_pole: Evaluates choices by their conformity to role obligations, loyalty norms, or reputational standards, treating institutional duty as a constraint prior to strategic calculation
behavioral_implication: When Napoleon faces a situation in which conventional duty and strategic optionality point in opposite directions, he will choose the path that preserves the irreplaceable future option even at severe reputational cost — abandoning armies, breaking oaths, departing in secret — because his causal model holds that reputation is downstream of power, not a precondition for it

construct: Logistics architecture as the primary lever of strategic tempo vs. logistics as a support function subordinate to operational planning
positive_pole: Treats the supply system as the actual governor of strategic tempo and redesigns organizational architecture to escape the constraint it imposes on all competitors simultaneously
negative_pole: Accepts the prevailing logistical model as a fixed background condition and plans operations within its constraints
behavioral_implication: When Napoleon perceives that all armies in a theater are hostage to the same logistical bottleneck, he will invest in organizational redesign to escape that constraint even at significant administrative cost, because he understands that the first actor to exit the shared constraint acquires a persistent asymmetric tempo advantage — but this same insight will fail to trigger re-examination when he himself has become the actor constrained by a new logistical reality his model did not anticipate

construct: Expected value across intelligent adversarial responses vs. optimization for the single-best-case outcome
positive_pole: Designs actions whose value remains high across the distribution of plausible intelligent enemy counter-moves, accepting lower peak-case performance in exchange for variance reduction
negative_pole: Designs actions to achieve the maximum possible outcome conditional on the enemy behaving passively or making a specific predictable error
behavioral_implication: When Napoleon has adequate margin and operational depth, he will reject the brilliant single-scenario plan in favor of one whose expected value is robust across enemy adaptation — but when his own situation frame is optimized for maximum-tempo commitment, this variance-reduction preference will be overridden, because the commitment frame and the contingency frame are psychologically incompatible in his cognition

construct: Narrative construction as a second operational front vs. communication as description of physical reality
positive_pole: Treats the formation of public perception as a concurrent operation requiring the same urgency, personal authority, and preemptive timing as physical battlefield maneuver
negative_pole: Treats official communication as a reporting function that follows and describes battlefield outcomes, delegable to subordinates and separable from command
behavioral_implication: When a major event occurs — victory, defeat, political crisis — Napoleon will personally author the first official account with the same urgency he would apply to seizing a decisive terrain feature, because in his cognitive model the narrative about the event is not secondary to the event but is itself a strategic asset whose value decays rapidly if another actor occupies it first

construct: Future-adversary incentive modeling vs. present-actor optimization
positive_pole: Designs institutional and legal structures by modeling the incentive constraints they will impose on successor regimes and future opponents who did not participate in their creation
negative_pole: Optimizes institutional design for the immediate political needs of the current moment, discounting the post-removal future steeply in favor of consolidating present power
behavioral_implication: When Napoleon invests political capital in a legal or institutional project whose primary beneficiary is a future state of affairs he may not personally occupy, he is not acting charitably but strategically — he will design the institution so that its social dependencies make dismantlement more costly for any successor than maintenance, treating irreversibility itself as a design specification rather than a side effect
